texts.label: text

texts.text: ANDREI ZLOTCHEVSKI 1 , † AND LINAN CHEN 1 , † , ∗

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Abstract. The unbalanced Schr¨ odinger bridge problem (uSBP) seeks to interpolate between a probability measure ρ 0 and a sub-probability measure ρ T while minimizing KL divergence to a reference measure R on a path space. In this work, we investigate the case where R is the path measure of a diffusion process with killing, which we interpret as a regime-switching diffusion. In addition to matching the initial and terminal distributions of trajectories that survive up to time T , we consider a general constraint ψ ( t, x ) on the distribution of killing times and/or killing locations.
texts.text: Abstract. The unbalanced Schr¨ odinger bridge problem (uSBP) seeks to interpolate between a probability measure ρ 0 and a sub-probability measure ρ T while minimizing KL divergence to a reference measure R on a path space. In this work, we investigate the case where R is the path measure of a diffusion process with killing, which we interpret as a regime-switching diffusion. In addition to matching the initial and terminal distributions of trajectories that survive up to time T , we consider a general constraint ψ ( t, x ) on the distribution of killing times and/or killing locations.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We investigate the uSBPs corresponding to four choices of ψ in detail which reflect different levels of information available to an observer. Wealso provide a rigorous analysis of the connections and the comparisons among the outcomes of these four cases. Our results are novel in the field of uSBP. The regime-switching approach proposed in this work provides a unified framework for tackling different uSBP scenarios, which not only reconciles but also extends the existing literature on uSBP.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 1. Introduction
texts.text: 1. Introduction

texts.label: text
texts.orig: This paper is dedicated to the unbalanced Schr¨ odinger bridge problem (uSBP) for diffusion processes. Roughly speaking, given probability distributions ρ 0 and ρ T the classic Schr¨ odinger bridge problem (SBP) aims to 'bridge' the two distributions in a way that minimizes KL divergence (relative entropy) to a reference path measure R . The unbalanced Schr¨ odinger bridge problem, on the other hand, considers the case where ρ T is a sub-probability measure (i.e. with total mass less than 1). In this Introduction, we shall describe the type of reference measure under consideration and then rigorously formulate the uSBP as a classic SBP with regime switching.
texts.text: This paper is dedicated to the unbalanced Schr¨ odinger bridge problem (uSBP) for diffusion processes. Roughly speaking, given probability distributions ρ 0 and ρ T the classic Schr¨ odinger bridge problem (SBP) aims to 'bridge' the two distributions in a way that minimizes KL divergence (relative entropy) to a reference path measure R . The unbalanced Schr¨ odinger bridge problem, on the other hand, considers the case where ρ T is a sub-probability measure (i.e. with total mass less than 1). In this Introduction, we shall describe the type of reference measure under consideration and then rigorously formulate the uSBP as a classic SBP with regime switching.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: There are two main objectives to this work. First, we seek to establish a fundamental theory of the diffusion uSBP that can model a broad range of constraints imposed on the distribution of killed paths. By applying the theory of the SBP for (jump) diffusions with regime switching [17], we will thoroughly investigate the uSBP for diffusions and derive many of its properties in full generality. Then, we aim to connect the existing uSBP literature [3, 5, 6] under the regime-switching framework adopted in this work and demonstrate how these different problems are directly related. In fact, our approach will allow us to recover the existing results from other uSBP research and to offer new insight on some aspects of the uSBP.

texts.orig: 1.1. Set-up of Our Model. Our model is set on a 'hybrid' state space R d ×{ ⊚ · , ⊗} over a finite time interval t ∈ [0 , T ]. Here, ⊚ · and ⊗ are called the regimes of the process, where ⊚ · is the 'active' regime and ⊗ is the 'dead' regime. Let Ω be a certain class, which will be specifically defined later, of c` adl` ag paths ω : t ∈ [0 , T ] ↦→ ( ω X ( t ) , ω Λ ( t )) ∈ R d ×{ ⊚ · , ⊗} , equipped with the Skorokhod metric. For every t ∈ [0 , T ], define ( X t , Λ t ) : Ω → R d ×{ ⊚ · , ⊗} to be the time projection such that, for every ω ∈ Ω, X t ( ω ) = ω X ( t ) gives the spatial position of the path, and Λ t ( ω ) = ω Λ ( t ) is the regime of the path. If F := B Ω is the Borel σ -algebra on Ω, then F is generated by the time projections { ( X t , Λ t ) } [0 ,T ] . Let {F t } [0 ,T ] be the natural filtration (or its usual augmentation) associated with { ( X t , Λ t ) } [0 ,T ] . Denote by P (Ω) the set of probability measures on (Ω , F ). For P ∈ P (Ω), to which we refer as a path measure , we say that { ( X t , Λ t ) } [0 ,T ] ( ω ) is the canonical process under P if ω ∈ Ω is sampled under P . Finally, for 0 ≤ t ≤ T , we write the distribution of ( X t , Λ t ) under P as P t , and call it the marginal distribution at time t ; similarly, the joint distribution of ( X 0 , Λ 0 ) and ( X T , Λ T ) under P is denoted by P 0 T .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: 1.2. Background and Motivation. In the classic SBP, a large number of particles is observed at times t = 0 and t = T to have spatial distributions ρ 0 and ρ T respectively. One must then find the 'most probable way' that the particles have evolved between the two observations, which is understood as finding the path measure ̂ P that minimizes the KL divergence to the reference measure (prior belief) R . In particular, the SBP for diffusion processes is a long-celebrated problem dating back to E. Schr¨ odinger's papers [15, 16] in the 1930s and it has generated a rich literature; a selection of works include [1, 2, 4, 912, 14]. In the unbalanced SBP, the number of particles observed at time T is expected to be less than that at time 0. Therefore, one must reconstruct the 'most likely paths' while accounting for the disappearance, or the killing, of the missing particles. The original work [3] on uSBP treated this problem by considering an augmentation of the state space to include an absorbing 'cemetery state' c . The killing of a particle was interpreted as a jump to c , and the problem was recast as a classic SBP over c` adl` ag paths in R d ∪ { c } . In this paper, we instead adopt a regime-switching approach to this problem, following the example in [17]. The advantage of our approach is two-fold: first, we can directly apply the general theory of the SBP for regime-switching (jump) diffusions developed in [17] to study the uSBP, and second, we have additional flexibility in the use of the killing time K and the spatial coordinate X K -for a killed particle, allowing us to model a variety of real-life situations with different types of observational data. We elaborate more on the latter point below.

We elaborate more on the latter point below.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Although, as in the classic SBP framework, we are only allowed to make two observations on the path: first at time 0, and then at time T , in our regime-switching setup, we are able to incorporate more information about the path than just what the endpoints can tell seemingly. This is due to the fact that any killed particle is frozen in 'time and space'. For example, if ψ ( t, x ) = x , then the spatial component X T not only gives the position at time T of a particle that survived , but also records the killing location of a dead particle. As a result, the terminal constraint ρ T ( · , ⊗ ) exactly prescribes the distribution of killing locations.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: As alluded to above, the types of constraints on the distribution of killed particles concerned in Scenarios (1), (2) and (3) have appeared in the uSBP literature [5, 6], but these works did not impose a target distribution ρ T ( · , ⊚ · ) for the surviving particles. The regime-switching approach will allow us to solve these uSBP scenarios in full generality. texts.orig: One purpose of Assumption (C) is to guarantee the well-definedness of our model. Namely, under Assumption (C) , we can always find a strong Markov reference measure R ∈ P (Ω), with any pre-set initial distribution, such that the canonical process { ( X t , Λ t ) } [0 ,T ] under R satisfy both the killing mechanism (1.1.1) and the SDE (1.1.2). Second, backed by the classical regularity theory for parabolic partial differential equations (PDEs), Assumption (C) is also sufficient for the existence of a positive transition density function under R within the active regime. Moreover, this transition density function is known to satisfy both the forward and the backward PDEs corresponding to the uSBP. All the above provides the foundation for our study of the dynamics of the Schr¨ odinger bridge ̂ P . We will be more specific on the implications of Assumption (C) in Section 2.1.

texts.text: Under the collective effect of Assumptions (A) , (B) , (C) , following the general theory developed in [17], we will be able to study the Schr¨ odinger bridge ̂ P using stochastic calculus and analytic tools. Let us now give an overview of our results.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Main Results. Our work is composed of two main parts. In the first part, we derive various fundamental properties of the unbalanced Schr¨ odinger bridge ̂ P for diffusion with killing by adapting the general theory from [17] to our uSBP setting. To begin, in Section 2, we discuss the aspects of ̂ P that apply to all choices of ψ , even beyond the four Scenarios outlined in Section 1.2. Notably, we show that ̂ P is the path measure of another diffusion with killing. This part of the result is centered around the pair of functions φ and ̂ φ defined in (2.1.6) and (2.1.7) which, as we discuss in Section 3, are solutions to the dynamic Schr¨ odinger system corresponding to the uSBP. Based on φ and ̂ φ , we present (in Theorem 2.2.1 ) the explicit formulation of the SDE associated with ̂ P in the active regime, along with the new killing rate, and we further obtain (in Theorem 2.2.2 ) the transition density function under ̂ P . We also derive the stochastic control formulation of the uSBP and its solution (in Definition 2.2.3 and Theorem 2.2.4 ). These are 'universal' properties of ̂ P : although they depend on ψ (through φ ), they do not vary from one choice of ψ to another.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In Section 3, we study facets of the uSBP that must be analyzed on a case-by-case basis depending on the specific choice of ψ ; although the expressions yielded in this section still depend explicitly on ψ , they vary from Scenario to Scenario. The results obtained in Scenarios (3) and (4) generalize those of the previous uSBP works [17] and [3] respectively. In our Scenario (3), we consider a time and space dependent killing rate V ( t, x ) in contrast with the space-homogeneous killing rate V ( t ) in [17]. In our Scenario (4), we obtain additional insight on the uSBP compared to [3], such as the operator approach to the dynamic Schr¨ odinger system and Kolmogorov forward equation.

texts.orig: In the second part, we analyze connections among our different Scenarios as well as relations to other uSBP works. In Section 4, we compare uSBP Scenarios in which ( R , ρ 0 , ρ T ) are the 'same', in the sense that b, σ, R 0 , V, ρ 0 , ρ T ( · , ⊚ · ) are identical, and R T ( · , ⊗ ) , ρ T ( · , ⊗ ) satisfy the diagram at the end of Section 1.3 across the Scenarios. We demonstrate that the Scenario with a 'stronger' constraint yields a Schr¨ odinger bridge with greater KL divergence to the reference measure; for example, Scenario (1) has a stronger constraint compared to Scenario (2), as R (1) T ( · , ⊗ ) and ρ (1) T ( · , ⊗ ) respectively have R (2) T ( · , ⊗ ) and ρ (2) T ( · , ⊗ ) as the marginal, and in this case we have KL( ̂ P (1) || R (1) ) ≥ KL( ̂ P (2) || R (2) ). We also determine (in Theorem 4.1.3 ) the conditions under which the above equality is achieved. In particular, we show that the equality holds for a unique choice of ρ T ( · , ⊗ ). These conditions take a simple form in the case where R 0 is a Dirac delta distribution.

texts.orig: In Section 5, we examine the connections of our uSBP model to the uSBP studied in [5]. The latter is a variant of our Scenario (1) where the constraint ̂ P T ( · , ⊗ ) = ρ T ( · , ⊗ ) is removed. We demonstrate (in Theorem 5.1.3 ) that the unbalanced Schr¨ odinger bridge in this case inherits properties from our Scenario (1) and that the Schr¨ odinger system solution satisfies g ( · , ⊚ · ) ≡ 1. This recovers the uSBP results obtained in [5], further illustrating the versatility of the regime-switching approach adopted in this work.

texts.orig: In the previous section, we have derived general results on the uSBP that hold for any choice of ψ and thus cover a wide range of uSBP scenarios. However, there remain certain aspects of the uSBP which need to be treated on a case-by-case basis. For example, the analogue of (2.2.3) in the dead regime cannot be stated in general terms because there is no 'universal equivalent' to (2.1.7) for ̂ φ ( t, x, ⊗ ). In this section, we will cover Scenarios (1), (2), (3), (4) as described in Section 1.2; other choices of ψ may be approached by the same techniques with some adaptations. We will continue working under Assumptions (A) , (B) , and (C) throughout this section.

texts.orig: In this section we discuss the relations among the four Scenarios presented in the previous sections. For the sake of clarity, in this section we use the superscript ' ( k ) ', for k ∈ { 1 , 2 , 3 , 4 } , in all the relevant quantities to indicate the Scenario to which they correspond. Again, all the discussions are conducted under Assumptions (A) , (B) , and (C) . We will also assume that the reference measure R is the same in all four Scenarios, in the sense that the initial distribution R 0 ( · , ⊚ · ), the drift and diffusion coefficients b ( t, x ) , σ ( t, x ) in the regime ⊚ · , as well as the killing rate V ( t, x ), are identical from Scenario to Scenario.

texts.orig: Recall that these four Scenarios represent different levels of information regarding the killed particles up to time T . This naturally leads to a comparison question in an environment where two or more observers have access to different levels of information. For example, suppose that Observer 1 has access to the full joint distribution of the killing locations and the killing times ρ (1) T ([ [ x, τ ] ] , ⊗ ) as in Scenario (1), and Observer 2 can only see the temporal distribution ρ (2) T ([ [ c , τ ] ] , ⊗ ) as in Scenario (2), with ρ (2) T ([ [ c , τ ] ] , ⊗ ) = ∫ R d ρ (1) T ([ [ x, τ ] ] , ⊗ ) dx for every τ ∈ (0 , T ]. What is the difference between their respective solutions ̂ P (1) and ̂ P (2) , obtained using the uSBP models described in the previous section? Will Observer 2 be able to use the uSBP setup in Scenario (1)?

texts.orig: Let us first present an informal, intuitive argument to motivate our results. The uSBP in Scenario (2) imposes less of a constraint on the terminal distribution than that in Scenario (1), and therefore we would expect the relation KL( ̂ P (2) || R (2) ) ≤ KL( ̂ P (1) || R (1) ). In fact, finding ̂ P (2) in Scenario (2) can be viewed as a 'SBP within a SBP': every joint distribution of the killing locations and the killing times imposed through the constraint ρ (1) T ([ [ x, τ ] ] , ⊗ ) yields a path measure ̂ P (1) , and we must choose the one minimizing KL divergence while having ∫ R d ρ (1) T ([ [ x, τ ] ] , ⊗ ) dx = ρ (2) T ([ [ c , τ ] ] , ⊗ ) for every τ ∈ (0 , T ]. Meanwhile, even though the terminal distribution ̂ P (2) T ( · , ⊗ ) only concerns the killing times, there exists an 'unobserved' joint distribution of killing locations and killing times 4 corresponding to ̂ P (2) . If we were then to choose ρ (1) T ( · , ⊗ ) to be precisely this joint distribution, it seems reasonable that the Schr¨ odinger bridge ̂ P (1) would be in some sense 'identical' to ̂ P (2) . We make this discussion precise in the following subsection.

Next, consider a mix of Scenarios (2) and (3) in the following way: at every t ∈ [0 , T ], the observer knows the amount of mass lost, but not its spatial distribution; further, at time T , the observer has access to the overall (cumulative) spatial distribution of killed particles. In other words, if ρ T ([ [ x, τ ] ] , ⊗ ) is the full spatio-temporal distribution of killed particles, the observation provides the two marginals ∫ T 0 ρ T ([ [ x, τ ] ] , ⊗ ) dτ and ∫ R d ρ T ([ [ x, τ ] ] , ⊗ ) dx . In the spirit of the SBP, the objective is to match these observations while minimizing KL divergence to a reference measure. As in the case with Scenario ( ⋆ ), our regime-switching approach cannot model this behavior directly, and some modifications are required. texts.orig: This flexibility is the result of a combination of two factors. First, although the dynamics of the augmented coordinates are only indirectly driven by the regime-switching diffusion, they are still part of its spatial dimension. Second, the general model of the SBP for regimeswitching (jump) diffusions in [17] allows ψ, ˜ ψ to depend on the spatial variable including the augmented dimensions. Therefore, the augmented coordinates not only can 'read' the information contained in the current state of the process ( t, X t ), but can also access the information contained in all the augmented coordinates themselves. For example, if we would like the augmented coordinate n to count the number of times that a particle has been killed, then we can initialize n = 0 at time t = 0 and set ψ ( t, [ [ x, n ] ]) = [ [ x, n + 1] ]. If we also want to count the number of revivals, then we can add a second augmented coordinate m , set m = 0 at time t = 0, and choose ψ ( t, [ [ x, n , m ] ]) = [[ x, n + 1 , m ] ] and ˜ ψ ( t, [ [ x, n , m ] ]) = [ [ x, n , m + 1] ]. By doing so, we can impose additional constraints in the uSBP through the target distribution ρ T in the augmented dimensions.
